Rodents

[NO_RODE_001]

Author
Affiliation

Anders Kolstad

The Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA)

Published

May 28, 2026

Recommended citation: Kolstad, A. 2026. Rodents [Smågnagere] (ID(s): NO_RODE_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_RODE_001

Metadata
Log
  • May 28, 2026 - Started work
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/lemen_linnell_cropped.jpg"))

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: Small rodents, like lemming and mice, are keystone species in aline ecosystems. They function as a trophic link between the primary producers and top predators. When population are high, they impose a large disturbance on the vegetation, but at the same time they fuel the higher troficl levels and ensure survival and breeding success for species like raptors and foxes. Rodent cycles have a frequency of about 3-4 years, and it is the amplitude (size or height) of these peaks that make up this indicator. Rodent cycles, both their regularity and their amplidude, are negatively affected by many known and unknown drivers. The most important antropogenic driver is climate change, with warmer and wetter winters causing rodent declines due to the effect that this has on the snow cover.

  2. Methods: Rodent numbers have been monitored from 1990 to 2020 using traps. A new national monitoring program was initiated in 2020 based on camera traps, but these data are not yet integrated with the existing time series, so the 2024 estimate is based on expert opinion, informed by various data sources.
    We get the data from the Norwegian Nature Index database (Nater and Eriksen 2025) where also the X100 reference values are stored. These represent the predicted mean number of rodents in peaks year over a 10 year period, given minimal anthropogenic influence. The reference values are also based on expert opinion, but informed by extensive empirical data.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that rodent numbers in peak years are as numerous, or more, than what we expect under the reference condition. Values decreasing toward zero indicates that the average rodent peaks are diminishing.

  4. Results and development needs: The indicator value for Norway as a whole in 2024 is 0.28 [95% CI = 0.26-0.30]. This is extremely low and reflects the deminisjong effect of rodent cycles on Norwegian alpine ecosystems. Development needs include an integration of the current monitoring time series, with the old time series based on trap monitoring.

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Smågnagere, som lemen og mus, er nøkkelarter i alpine økosystemer. De fungerer som et trofisk bindeledd mellom primærprodusenter og toppredatorer. Når bestandene er høye, påfører de vegetasjonen betydelige forstyrrelser, men samtidig opprettholdes de høyere trofiske nivåene og smågnagerne sikrer overlevelse og hekkesuksess for arter som rovfugler og rever. Smågnagersyklusene har en frekvens på omtrent 3–4 år, og det er amplituden (størrelsen eller høyden) på toppårene som utgjør denne indikatoren. Smågnagersyklusene, både regelmessigheten og amplituden, påvirkes negativt av mange kjente og ukjente faktorer. Den viktigste menneskeskapte påvirkningsfaktoren er klimaendringer, der varmere og våtere vintre fører til nedgang i smågnagerbestandene på grunn av effektene dette har på snødekket.

  2. Metoder: Smågnagerbestander har blitt overvåket fra 1990 til 2020 ved hjelp av feller. Et nytt nasjonalt overvåkingsprogram ble startet i 2020 basert på kamerafeller, men disse dataene er ennå ikke integrert med de eksisterende tidsseriene. Estimatet for 2024 er derfor basert på ekspertvurderinger informert av ulike datakilder. Dataene hentes fra databasen for Naturindeks for Norge (Nater and Eriksen 2025), hvor også referanseverdiene X100 er lagret. Disse representerer det forventede gjennomsnittlige antallet smågnagere i toppår over en 10-årsperiode under minimale menneskeskapte påvirkninger. Referanseverdiene er også basert på ekspertvurderinger, men støttes av omfattende empiriske data.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at smågnagerbestandene i toppår er like store, eller større, enn det vi forventer under referansetilstanden. Verdier som nærmer seg null indikerer at toppene i smågnagersyklusene blir svakere.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: Indikatorverdien for hele Norge i 2024 er 0,28 [95 % KI = 0,26–0,30]. Dette er svært lavt og gjenspeiler den reduserte effekten smågnagersyklusene har på norske alpine økosystemer. Utviklingsbehov inkluderer integrering av de nåværende overvåkingsseriene med de eldre tidsseriene basert på felleovervåking.

2. About the underlying data

The data comes from various trap minitoring time series, especially TOV (Framstad 2017) and selected time series from (Ehrich et al. 2020). The 2024 estimate is based on expert opinion (Nater and Eriksen 2025) . See further documentation on the Norwegian Nature Index web site for this indicator.

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the different assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1950 (expert opinion-based estimate), 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019 and 2024 (also expert opinion-based).

2.2 Sampling design

WHen empirical data is ised, these are mostly from trap monitoring time series. Some qualitative data sources are used as well. See more information on the Naturindeks web site and the workflow documentation found here.

2.3 Original units

The original units of the raw data is the average size of population peaks over a 10 year period, meased as captured per 100 capture-days.

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Indicator values from the Norwegian Nature Index can be found in (Nater et al. 2025). Indicator values, variable values and reference values can be downloaded from the Nature Index database using the API, as I have done below. The data is licenced under CC-BY 4.0.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B3 - Functional state characteristics

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The ecosystem characteristic represented in this indicator is an ecosystem where ecosystem functions are stable (or at least predictable) and intact, and where tropic interactions occur as they would uder the reference condition.

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

Since rodents are functional links between so many species and trophic levels, it is assumed to be a strongly correlated to several other indicator, such as a potential snow depth indicator, and the arctic fox indicator (NO_AFOX_001).

3.4 Impact factors

Alpine rodents are influced by both natural (known and unknown) factors, such as predation, as well as antropogenic drivers, of which climate change is the most important. Wetter and warmer winters cause the snow cover to become thinner, less stable and less insulation, which negatively affects survaval and fitness in general.

3.5 Spatial aggregation

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition is a moutain ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the period 1961-1990 (Nybø and Evju (red) 2017).

4.2 Reference levels

X100 is defined based on the predicted size of rodent peaks. It is quantified from expert opinion, but informed from empirical data.

X0 is defined as a complete absence of rodents.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

The reference values vary between 10 regions in Norway.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

One main source of uncertainty arises from the discontinuous time series, since the minitoring switched from trap minitoring to camera monitoring in 2020. There is also an issue with low spatial representation in the data pre 2020.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The reported uncertainties reflect the uncertainties around the true parameter means, i.e. they are inferrential uncertainties for the true value of the indicator for the given area and time combination.

6. References

Ehrich, D., N. M. Schmidt, G. Gauthier, R. Alisauskas, A. Angerbjörn, K. Clark, F. Ecke, N. E. Eide, et al. 2020. Documenting lemming population change in the arctic: Can we detect trends? Ambio 49. Springer: 786–800.
Framstad, E. 2017. Terrestrisk naturovervåking i 2016: Markvegetasjon, epifytter, smågnagere og fugl. Sammenfatning av resultater. NINA Rapport 1376. Edited by Erik Framstad. Norsk institutt for naturforskning (NINA).
Nater, C. R., and L. F. Eriksen. 2025. Naturindeks for Norge 2025. Tilstand og utvikling for biologisk mangfold. {NINA} {Temahefte} 97. Trondheim: Norsk Institutt for Naturforskning.
Nater, C. R., T. Zeiner-Henriksen, and S. Nybø. 2025. NI2025: Indicator Data. doi:10.21345/y790-v762.
Nybø, S., and M. Evju (red). 2017. Fagsystem for fastsetting av god økologisk tilstand - forslag fra et ekspertråd.

7. Import and prepare datasets

7.1 Naturindeks

We download the dataset from the Naturindeks database using the API. The function giver read-only access without requiring a username and password.

Code 
# I use a dev version of NIcalc where the username and passwork for the guest user is hrd coded into the function. This will probably be merged into the real NIcalc (from NINAnor/NIcalc) soon.
# renv::install("ChloeRN/NIcalc@include_ReadOnly_access")
# library(NIcalc)

#myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2020, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))

# Import data set
roedImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Smågnagere - fjellbestander"),
  year = c("2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)
saveRDS(roedImport, here::here("data/roedImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

According to the Naturindeks terminology, the 430 municipalities are called BSunits. In the current dataset, only 163 mountain municipalities are included.

The indicator data is available at the scale of 10 ICunits. These are unique contellation of municipalities and/or counties.

The five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are called parts and NIunits. We can think of the ICuntis as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

9. Analyses

Here I use a generic approach to handling data from the Naturindeks where I get both the variable values and the reference values, separately, from the NI database, and perform the normalisation and spatial aggregation ourselves.

The reference values have uncertainties attached to them, which we will ignore here. The variable values have uncertainties as well, in the form of probability function. The uncertainties are from a statistical model, and so we think of them as inferential uncertainties.

Here I use an NIcalc function to just prepare and structure the data a little differently.

Code 
# Assemble
assemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = roedImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Then I get the reference values. These are unique to each ´ICunit`. I only have an X100 value. X0 is a natural zero.

Code 
x100 <- roedImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  select(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
gt::gt(x100)
ICunitId ICunitName x100
5458 Hedmark 10
5459 Oppland 15
5460 Buskerud, Telemark 20
5461 Agder, Rogaland 20
5462 Hordaland 20
5463 Sogn og Fjordane - Møre 15
5465 Midt-Norge, nord 15
5466 Midt-Norge, sør 15
5467 Nordland-Troms 20
5468 Nord-Troms, Finnmark 20

The variable values are stored as a parameterised probability function, and I need to draw n samples from this function. The probability function differs between indicators in the Naturindeks. It could also differ within an indicator. Here I create a vector defining which probability function to use. I use ´tradObs´ where there is a regular probability distribution family, and customObs where customDist!= NA. When distributionFamilyName == NA, customDistribution != NA.

Code 
myYears <- as.character(c(2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))

obstype <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  obs <- assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName

  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"

  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}

Now I am ready to sample the probability distributions. I set n to be 1000.

Code 
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  #print(i)
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  
  myMat <-  myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId")) |>
    select(-name) |>
    add_column(year = myYears[i])
  
  dat <- dat |> 
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat)
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  ICunitId  value year 
  <chr>     <dbl> <chr>
1 5458     5.18   2010 
2 5458     0.614  2010 
3 5458     6.01   2010 
4 5458     0.0710 2010 
5 5458     2.70   2010 
6 5458     2.08   2010

This is the point in the spatial aggregation where we should normalise against the reference values. So let’s add inn the X100 values.

Code 
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

Then we can normalise also the scatter plot with variables against indicator values (Figure 2).

Code 
ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x100 = dat$x100
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)
Code 
myMax <- max(dat$x100)
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  labs(x = "Avg. peak size") +
  xlim(c(0,myMax))

ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng_roed.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng_roed.png"))

I also export a figure for the Norwegian fact sheet:

Export normalisation function plots 
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  labs(x = "Størrelse på bestandstopper", y = "Indikatorverdi") +
  xlim(c(0, myMax))
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_facet_roed.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)

Prepare for aggregation

Then we estimate values for each geographic region (N, M, E, S, W). In Naturindeks this is done behind the scenes, and I’m not sure how.

I will downscale the indicator values from ICunits to BSunits. This is because these are not perfectly netsed. ICunit 5461 for example, contains Rogaland and Agder, but these belong to different NIunits. Based on the alpine area of each BSunit, I will upscale from BSunits to NIunits.

Code 
# The link between BSunits and NIunits can be found here:
bs_ni <- as.data.frame(assemble$NIunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits")
head(bs_ni)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N
1       Hamar         1 1 0 0 0 0
2   Ringsaker         1 1 0 0 0 0
3      Trysil         1 1 0 0 0 0
4        Åmot         1 1 0 0 0 0
5 Stor-Elvdal         1 1 0 0 0 0
6    Rendalen         1 1 0 0 0 0
Code 
# And the link between BSunits and ICunits can be found here:
bs_ic <- as.data.frame(assemble$ICunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits") |>
  rename(ICunits = "Smågnagere - fjellbestander")
head(bs_ic)
      BSunits ICunits
1       Hamar    5458
2   Ringsaker    5458
3      Trysil    5458
4        Åmot    5458
5 Stor-Elvdal    5458
6    Rendalen    5458
Code 
# and then I combine them
bs_ni_ic <- bs_ni |>
  left_join(bs_ic, by = join_by(BSunits))
head(bs_ni_ic)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N ICunits
1       Hamar         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
2   Ringsaker         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
3      Trysil         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
4        Åmot         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
5 Stor-Elvdal         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
6    Rendalen         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458
Code 
# and we also get the alpine area
bs_fjell <- as.data.frame(assemble$BSunits) |>
  select(BSunits = name, Fjell) |>
  mutate(BSunits = as.character(BSunits))
head(bs_fjell)
       BSunits     Fjell
43       Hamar  13.57110
44   Ringsaker  42.62685
54      Trysil 254.97747
55        Åmot  32.34474
56 Stor-Elvdal 385.84455
57    Rendalen 800.18657
Code 
# and add that as well
bs_ni_ic2 <- bs_ni_ic |>
  left_join(bs_fjell, by = join_by(BSunits)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunits))
head(bs_ni_ic2)
      BSunits wholeArea E S W C N ICunits     Fjell ICunitId
1       Hamar         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458  13.57110     5458
2   Ringsaker         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458  42.62685     5458
3      Trysil         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458 254.97747     5458
4        Åmot         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458  32.34474     5458
5 Stor-Elvdal         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458 385.84455     5458
6    Rendalen         1 1 0 0 0 0    5458 800.18657     5458
Code 
# and add the new columns to the main dataset
head(dat)
# A tibble: 6 × 6
  ICunitId  value year  ICunitName  x100 indicator
  <chr>     <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>
1 5458     5.18   2010  Hedmark       10   0.518  
2 5458     0.614  2010  Hedmark       10   0.0614 
3 5458     6.01   2010  Hedmark       10   0.601  
4 5458     0.0710 2010  Hedmark       10   0.00710
5 5458     2.70   2010  Hedmark       10   0.270  
6 5458     2.08   2010  Hedmark       10   0.208
Code 
dat_expand <- dat |>
  right_join(
    bs_ni_ic2,
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )
head(dat_expand)
# A tibble: 6 × 15
  ICunitId value year  ICunitName  x100 indicator BSunits  wholeArea     E     S
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Hamar            1     1     0
2 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Ringsak…         1     1     0
3 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Trysil           1     1     0
4 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Åmot             1     1     0
5 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Stor-El…         1     1     0
6 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Rendalen         1     1     0
# ℹ 5 more variables: W <dbl>, C <dbl>, N <dbl>, ICunits <int>, Fjell <dbl>
Code 
# and we create a column for the regions
dat_expand2 <- dat_expand |>
  select(-wholeArea) |>
  mutate(across(c(E, S, W, C, N), ~ na_if(., 0))) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(E, S, W, C, N), values_drop_na = T, values_to = "count", names_to = "part") |>
  select(-count)
head(dat_expand2)
# A tibble: 6 × 10
  ICunitId value year  ICunitName  x100 indicator BSunits    ICunits Fjell part 
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr> <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>        <int> <dbl> <chr>
1 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Hamar         5458  13.6 E    
2 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Ringsaker     5458  42.6 E    
3 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Trysil        5458 255.  E    
4 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Åmot          5458  32.3 E    
5 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Stor-Elvd…    5458 386.  E    
6 5458      5.18 2010  Hedmark       10     0.518 Rendalen      5458 800.  E

Spatial aggregation

Then we can aggregate from municipalities to geographic regions using ecTools. This uses a Monte Carlo propagation of the uncertainties.

This takes a while to run with n = 1000.

Code 
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = dat_expand2,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Fjell,
  start_units = BSunits,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/mc_propagated.rds"))

To get the national indicator estimate, we will do another MC propagation from geographic regions to national scale. So we need the summed mountain area per geographic region.

Code 
aux <- dat_expand2 |>
  select(-indicator) |>
  distinct(BSunits, .keep_all = T) |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(alpine_area = sum(Fjell)) 
aux
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  part  alpine_area
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 C          12343.
2 E          17917.
3 N          52485.
4 S           6529.
5 W          13041.
Code 
mc_propagated <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated2 <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = alpine_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated2, here::here("data/mc_propagated2.rds"))

Then we append this to the main dataset

Code 
mc_propagated3 <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated2)

10. Results

Then we create some summary statistics (Table 2) and plot them (Figure 3).

Code 
summary_out <- mc_propagated3 |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975), 2),
  ) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B1",
    Indicator = "Rodents",
    Ecosystem = "Mountains",
    ID = "NO_RODE_001"
  )
Code 
part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)

region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")

region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(region_levels)),
  region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = myYears),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region"),
    part2 = recode(part, !!!part_labels)
  )
Code 
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part2, group = part2)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 2, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(
      nrow = 2,
      override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)
    ),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
Code 
summary_out |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  knitr::kable()

11. Export file

The indicator values are exported and can be found under data/results_NO_RODE_001_v1.0.0.csv.

Export data 
write.csv(mc_propagated3, here::here("data/results_NO_RODE_001_v1.0.0.csv"), row.names = F)

12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Nater and Eriksen 2025).

Session Info

This workflow uses renv. Run renv::restore() to recreate the working environment.
